title: Somatic Experiencing for Psychic Phenomena

body:
Somatic Experiencing for Psychic Phenomena

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. Maybe really late. You've been scrolling, searching, trying to make sense of what's happening to you. That buzz under your skin, the weird visions, the sudden terror that just grips you out of nowhere. You feel like you're losing your mind, right? Like you've opened a door you can't close. I know that feeling. I've been there, staring at the ceiling at 3 AM, wondering if I was going to crack. Let me tell you, you're not alone. And you're not crazy. What you're experiencing, these "psychic phenomena" or spiritual emergencies - they're real. And Somatic Experiencing, believe it or not, can be a lifeline.

Look, when you're going through something intense like a kundalini awakening or a spiritual emergency, your body becomes a battlefield. It's not just "all in your head." Your nervous system is screaming. It's overloaded. It's firing off signals that it's in mortal danger, even if there's no visible threat. That's where Somatic Experiencing (SE) comes in. It's not about talking your way out of it, not primarily. It's about helping your body process the sheer overwhelm, the raw energy, the terror that's trapped in your cells. It's about working with the animal part of you, the part that just wants to flee, fight, or freeze. And believe me, that part's working overtime right now, isn't it?

I've seen it, lived it. The feeling of energy coursing through you, electric shocks, spontaneous movements, visions that feel more real than reality. It's disorienting. It can be terrifying. You try to explain it to friends, family, even some therapists, and you get blank stares or worse, suggestions for medication that don't quite hit the mark. Because what's happening isn't just a psychological issue. It's deeply physical, deeply energetic. It's your system trying to process something massive, something it wasn't prepared for. And often, it's bringing up old, unresolved trauma - stuff you didn't even know was there - because these awakenings, they're like a cosmic vacuum cleaner, sucking up everything that needs to be cleared. Right?

The Body's Scream - Why Talk Therapy Isn't Enough

When you're in the throes of a spiritual emergency, your system is in a constant state of fight, flight, or freeze. Your sympathetic nervous system is stuck on high alert. You might feel a constant tremor, a buzzing, a sense of dread, or an inability to relax. You might have panic attacks that come out of nowhere. Your sleep might be shot. Your digestion might be a mess. These aren't just symptoms; they're your body's way of telling you it's overwhelmed. It's screaming for help, but it's speaking a language of sensation, not words.

Traditional talk therapy, while helpful for some things, often misses this crucial piece. You can talk about your visions, your existential dread, your sense of cosmic connection or cosmic terror all day long, but if your body is still locked in a survival response, those words won't calm it down. It's like trying to reason with a terrified animal. You need to speak its language. You need to help it discharge the trapped energy, the unresolved survival responses that are fueling the fire.

Think about it. When an animal in the wild escapes a predator, what does it do? It shakes. It trembles. It discharges all that adrenaline, all that trapped energy. It completes the survival response. But we humans, we often don't. We suppress it. We intellectualize it. We tell ourselves we should "be strong" or "get over it." And when a powerful energetic awakening hits, all that suppressed energy, all those incomplete survival responses, they can get stirred up. They can feel like a new, terrifying phenomenon, but often, they're just old stuff finally coming to the surface, demanding to be seen and released.

The Overwhelm of Psychic Phenomena

You might be experiencing things that defy explanation. Spontaneous movements (kriyas), internal sounds, flashes of light, a sense of being watched, precognitive dreams, telepathic experiences - the list goes on. These can be beautiful, yes, but they can also be incredibly destabilizing, especially if they're coming at you fast and hard, without any context or control. It's like having your senses turned up to 11, but you don't have a volume knob. Know what I mean?

Your nervous system interprets this influx of unusual sensory information, this raw energetic input, as a threat. Because it is a threat to its established equilibrium. It doesn't know how to categorize it. It doesn't have a "psychic phenomena" folder in its brain. So it defaults to its most primitive response: danger. And that's when the terror kicks in. That's when you feel like you're spiraling. That's when you start to question your sanity. You're not making it up. Your body is genuinely reacting to an new level of stimulation.

I've heard countless stories, and lived some of them, where the sheer intensity of these experiences leads to deep isolation. You can't talk about it without fear of judgment, or being told you're psychotic. But the truth is, many people go through this. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is a classic for a reason - it validates these experiences as real, not just pathology. You're not broken. You're just experiencing a radical shift, and your system needs help integrating it, not suppressing it.

How Somatic Experiencing Helps You Re-regulate

So, what does SE actually do? It's not about "fixing" you. It's about helping your body remember its natural capacity for self-regulation. It's about gently guiding your nervous system out of that stuck fight/flight/freeze response. It does this by working with sensation, not just narrative.

  • Tracking Sensation: An SE practitioner will help you pay attention to subtle bodily sensations - not just the big, scary ones, but also the neutral or even pleasant ones. This helps you develop a sense of curiosity about your internal world, rather than just being overwhelmed by it.
  • Titration: This is a key principle. Instead of diving headfirst into the most terrifying sensations (which can re-traumatize you), you work in small, manageable doses. You touch on a difficult sensation for a moment, then return to a neutral or resourced feeling. It's like dipping your toe in cold water, rather than jumping in all at once. This builds your capacity to tolerate intense feelings without being flooded.
  • Pendulation: You'll learn to move between states of activation (those intense, uncomfortable feelings) and states of calm or resource. This helps your nervous system learn that it can move out of high alert, that it's not stuck there forever. It's like a gentle rocking motion, back and forth, building resilience.
  • Discharging Trapped Energy: As your nervous system starts to feel safer, it can begin to release the trapped energy from past traumas or the current overwhelm. This might manifest as trembling, heat, cold, tingling, yawning, sighing, or a sudden wave of emotion. These are all signs that your body is completing its natural healing cycle.
  • Resourcing: You'll identify and strengthen internal and external resources that help you feel safe and grounded. This could be a memory of a peaceful place, a feeling of warmth in your hands, the support of a friend, or simply the feeling of your feet on the ground. These resources become anchors when the waves of sensation get too big.

When you're dealing with kundalini energy, for example, it can feel like a massive, uncontrolled force. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) describes many of these intense bodily experiences. SE doesn't try to stop the energy, but rather helps your system learn to contain it, to integrate it, so it doesn't feel so chaotic and threatening. It helps your nervous system feel like it has some agency, some control, even when the energy itself feels overwhelming. Does that land?

Grounding and Containment: Your New Best Friends

Honestly, when you're going through this, grounding and containment aren't just good ideas, they're survival skills. These are things SE emphasizes, and they're crucial when psychic phenomena feel like they're tearing you apart.

  • Physical Grounding: This means connecting with the physical world. Feel your feet on the floor. Pay attention to the chair supporting you. Notice the clothes on your skin. These simple acts bring your awareness back to your body, back to the present moment, out of the swirling chaos in your head or the intense internal sensations.
  • Environmental Grounding: Look around you. Name five things you can see, four things you can touch, three things you can hear, two things you can smell, one thing you can taste. This is a classic anxiety technique, and it works because it pulls your attention away from internal overwhelm and into your immediate, safe surroundings.
  • Containment: This is about creating boundaries for the intense energy or sensations. Imagine a container around you - a safe space where you can experience what's happening without feeling like it's going to swallow you whole. You can visualize this, or you can physically create it by holding your hands over your solar plexus, or wrapping your arms around yourself. It's a way of telling your nervous system, "Okay, this is happening, but I'm safe within these boundaries."

I've personally used these techniques countless times when the energy was too much, when the visions were too vivid, when the terror was too real. They don't make the phenomena disappear, but they make them bearable. They create a little bit of space, a little bit of safety, so your system can start to process instead of just react in panic. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) both talk about the importance of practical, grounded approaches to these experiences, and SE fits right in with that wisdom.

Finding a Practitioner and Trusting Your Gut

This is important. Not all SE practitioners are familiar with spiritual emergencies or psychic phenomena. When you're looking for someone, don't be afraid to ask. Ask if they have experience with "spiritual emergence," "kundalini awakening," or "non-ordinary states of consciousness." Some will, some won't. You need someone who won't pathologize your experience, who won't tell you it's "just anxiety" or try to medicate away something that's clearly more than that. You need someone who can hold space for the weird, the wild, the terrifying, without judgment.

Here's the thing: you're already incredibly sensitive. Your intuition is probably screaming at you, even if you don't trust it right now. Use that. When you talk to a potential practitioner, pay attention to how your body feels. Do you feel a slight softening? A sense of ease? Or do you feel more tense, more guarded? Trust that gut feeling. It's your body telling you if this person feels safe enough to help you regulate.

It won't be a quick fix. This isn't a magic pill. It's a process of slow, gentle, persistent work with your nervous system. There will be good days and bad days. There will be moments of intense release and moments of deep frustration. But here's what I can promise you: by working with your body, by giving it the tools to process what's happening, you can move from feeling like a victim of these phenomena to feeling like you have some agency, some capacity to integrate them. You can learn to ride the waves, instead of being drowned by them.

So yeah, you're not crazy. You're going through something intense, something real. And there are ways to work with it, ways to bring your body back online, ways to find some peace amidst the storm. Keep searching, keep asking, keep listening to that inner voice. You've got this, even if it doesn't feel like it right now. One breath at a time, one sensation at a time. That's how you get through it.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
